Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended

Air Quality, Forecasts & Multi-City Comparisons

Author

Immanuel Williams

Overview

You already have your OpenWeather API key from Session 3. This lab takes you further into the same API — exploring endpoints you haven’t used yet and building toward multi-city comparisons.

You will use:

  • Air Quality Index (AQI) — requires coordinates (lat/lon), not just a city name
  • 5-Day Forecast — 40 time-step nested response across 5 days
  • Multi-city pipeline — map a function across a vector of cities

Auth pattern: Same as Sessions 3–4 — API key as appid query parameter.

Step 1 — Setup

library(httr2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(glue)
library(dotenv)

load_dot_env()
weather_key <- Sys.getenv("OPENWEATHER_KEY")

Exercise 1: Air Quality Index

The AQI endpoint requires latitude and longitude, not a city name. This means you need to geocode first — exactly the pattern from Session 3.

Step 1a — Geocode a city

get_coordinates <- function(city, key) {
  url <- glue("http://api.openweathermap.org/geo/1.0/direct?q={city}&limit=1&appid={key}")
  result <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(url)))
  list(lat = result[[1]]$lat, lon = result[[1]]$lon)
}

coords <- get_coordinates("San Luis Obispo", weather_key)
cat("Lat:", coords$lat, "| Lon:", coords$lon, "\n")

Step 1b — Fetch air quality

aqi_url <- glue(
  "http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/air_pollution",
  "?lat={coords$lat}&lon={coords$lon}&appid={weather_key}"
)

response <- req_perform(request(aqi_url))
aqi_data <- resp_body_json(response)

# AQI scale: 1=Good, 2=Fair, 3=Moderate, 4=Poor, 5=Very Poor
aqi_value  <- aqi_data$list[[1]]$main$aqi
components <- aqi_data$list[[1]]$components

cat("Air Quality Index:", aqi_value, "\n")
cat("PM2.5:", components$pm2_5, "μg/m³\n")
cat("CO:",    components$co,    "μg/m³\n")
cat("NO2:",   components$no2,   "μg/m³\n")
cat("O3:",    components$o3,    "μg/m³\n")

Exercise 2: 5-Day Forecast

The forecast endpoint returns 40 time steps — one every 3 hours over 5 days. The data lives inside a list key (an array).

forecast_url <- glue(
  "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/forecast",
  "?q=San+Luis+Obispo&appid={weather_key}&units=imperial"
)

response      <- req_perform(request(forecast_url))
forecast_data <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Number of time steps:", length(forecast_data$list), "\n")
cat("City:", forecast_data$city$name, ",", forecast_data$city$country, "\n")
# Flatten the nested list into a tidy data frame
forecast_df <- lapply(forecast_data$list, function(step) {
  data.frame(
    datetime    = as_datetime(step$dt),
    temp        = step$main$temp,
    feels_like  = step$main$feels_like,
    humidity    = step$main$humidity,
    description = step$weather[[1]]$description,
    wind_speed  = step$wind$speed,
    pop         = step$pop  # probability of precipitation
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

glimpse(forecast_df)
ggplot(forecast_df, aes(x = datetime, y = temp)) +
  geom_line(color = "#4f46e5", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = temp - 3, ymax = temp + 3), alpha = 0.15, fill = "#6366f1") +
  geom_point(aes(color = pop), size = 2) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#a5b4fc", high = "#ef4444",
                       name = "Rain Prob.") +
  labs(
    title    = "5-Day Temperature Forecast — San Luis Obispo",
    subtitle = "Point color indicates probability of precipitation",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Temperature (°F)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

Exercise 3: Compare Multiple Cities

Write a reusable function, then map it across a list of cities. This is the core pattern for any multi-source data pipeline.

get_current_weather <- function(city, key) {
  url <- glue(
    "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather",
    "?q={city}&appid={key}&units=imperial"
  )

  response <- req_perform(request(url))

  if (resp_status(response) != 200) {
    message("Failed for city: ", city)
    return(NULL)
  }

  d <- resp_body_json(response)

  data.frame(
    city        = d$name,
    country     = d$sys$country,
    temp        = d$main$temp,
    feels_like  = d$main$feels_like,
    humidity    = d$main$humidity,
    description = d$weather[[1]]$description,
    wind_speed  = d$wind$speed
  )
}
cities <- c(
  "San Luis Obispo,US",
  "Chicago,US",
  "Miami,US",
  "Seattle,US",
  "Denver,US"
)

# Map the function across all cities
weather_comparison <- lapply(cities, get_current_weather, key = weather_key) |>
  bind_rows()

print(weather_comparison)
ggplot(weather_comparison, aes(x = reorder(city, temp), y = temp, fill = temp)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, radius = unit(4, "pt")) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(temp), "°F")),
            hjust = -0.15, fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#4f46e5", high = "#ef4444") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Current Temperature by City",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Temperature (°F)",
    fill  = "Temp (°F)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Questions

———————

Q1.

(Conceptual): The AQI endpoint requires lat/lon, not a city name. Why do you think some API endpoints work with city names while others require coordinates?

TipAnswer Q1

City names are ambiguous — there are many cities named “Springfield” or “Paris.” Coordinates are globally unique. Endpoints that measure physical phenomena at a precise geographic point (like air quality or elevation) require coordinates because a city-level approximation would be inaccurate. The geocoding step converts the human-readable name into the precise coordinates the scientific endpoint needs.

———————

———————

Q2.

(Code): The 5-day forecast datetime column comes from a Unix timestamp (step$dt). What does as_datetime() from lubridate do to it, and what would the raw value look like without that conversion?

TipAnswer Q2

as_datetime() converts the raw Unix timestamp (an integer counting seconds since January 1, 1970) into a human-readable POSIXct datetime object. Without conversion, the column would contain numbers like 1704067200 — correct but unreadable and unusable for time-based plotting.

———————

———————

Q3.

(Extension): Modify get_current_weather() to also retrieve the air quality index for each city. You’ll need to geocode the city first, then call the AQI endpoint with the coordinates. What challenges do you run into?

TipAnswer Q3

The main challenge is making two API calls per city instead of one — geocoding (to get lat/lon) and then AQI (using those coordinates). This doubles the number of requests and makes rate limiting more of a concern. A robust solution caches the coordinates and handles errors at each step:

get_weather_and_aqi <- function(city, key) {
  coords <- get_coordinates(city, key)
  weather <- get_current_weather(city, key)

  aqi_url  <- glue("http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/air_pollution?lat={coords$lat}&lon={coords$lon}&appid={key}")
  aqi_resp <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(aqi_url)))

  weather$aqi <- aqi_resp$list[[1]]$main$aqi
  weather
}

———————

Source Code

title: "Practice Lab 3: OpenWeather Extended"
subtitle: "Air Quality, Forecasts & Multi-City Comparisons"
author: "Immanuel Williams"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    theme: cosmo
    css: ../../custom.css
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)
```

## Overview

You already have your OpenWeather API key from **Session 3**. This lab takes you further into the same API — exploring endpoints you haven't used yet and building toward multi-city comparisons.

You will use:

- **Air Quality Index (AQI)** — requires coordinates (lat/lon), not just a city name
- **5-Day Forecast** — 40 time-step nested response across 5 days
- **Multi-city pipeline** — map a function across a vector of cities

**Auth pattern:** Same as Sessions 3–4 — API key as `appid` query parameter.

---

## Step 1 — Setup

```{r load-libs}
library(httr2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(glue)
library(dotenv)

load_dot_env()
weather_key <- Sys.getenv("OPENWEATHER_KEY")
```

---

## Exercise 1: Air Quality Index

The AQI endpoint requires **latitude and longitude**, not a city name. This means you need to geocode first — exactly the pattern from Session 3.

### Step 1a — Geocode a city

```{r geocode}
get_coordinates <- function(city, key) {
  url <- glue("http://api.openweathermap.org/geo/1.0/direct?q={city}&limit=1&appid={key}")
  result <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(url)))
  list(lat = result[[1]]$lat, lon = result[[1]]$lon)
}

coords <- get_coordinates("San Luis Obispo", weather_key)
cat("Lat:", coords$lat, "| Lon:", coords$lon, "\n")
```

### Step 1b — Fetch air quality

```{r aqi-request}
aqi_url <- glue(
  "http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/air_pollution",
  "?lat={coords$lat}&lon={coords$lon}&appid={weather_key}"
)

response <- req_perform(request(aqi_url))
aqi_data <- resp_body_json(response)

# AQI scale: 1=Good, 2=Fair, 3=Moderate, 4=Poor, 5=Very Poor
aqi_value  <- aqi_data$list[[1]]$main$aqi
components <- aqi_data$list[[1]]$components

cat("Air Quality Index:", aqi_value, "\n")
cat("PM2.5:", components$pm2_5, "μg/m³\n")
cat("CO:",    components$co,    "μg/m³\n")
cat("NO2:",   components$no2,   "μg/m³\n")
cat("O3:",    components$o3,    "μg/m³\n")
```

---

## Exercise 2: 5-Day Forecast

The forecast endpoint returns 40 time steps — one every 3 hours over 5 days. The data lives inside a `list` key (an array).

```{r forecast-request}
forecast_url <- glue(
  "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/forecast",
  "?q=San+Luis+Obispo&appid={weather_key}&units=imperial"
)

response      <- req_perform(request(forecast_url))
forecast_data <- resp_body_json(response)

cat("Number of time steps:", length(forecast_data$list), "\n")
cat("City:", forecast_data$city$name, ",", forecast_data$city$country, "\n")
```

```{r forecast-flatten}
# Flatten the nested list into a tidy data frame
forecast_df <- lapply(forecast_data$list, function(step) {
  data.frame(
    datetime    = as_datetime(step$dt),
    temp        = step$main$temp,
    feels_like  = step$main$feels_like,
    humidity    = step$main$humidity,
    description = step$weather[[1]]$description,
    wind_speed  = step$wind$speed,
    pop         = step$pop  # probability of precipitation
  )
}) |> bind_rows()

glimpse(forecast_df)
```

```{r forecast-plot}
ggplot(forecast_df, aes(x = datetime, y = temp)) +
  geom_line(color = "#4f46e5", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = temp - 3, ymax = temp + 3), alpha = 0.15, fill = "#6366f1") +
  geom_point(aes(color = pop), size = 2) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "#a5b4fc", high = "#ef4444",
                       name = "Rain Prob.") +
  labs(
    title    = "5-Day Temperature Forecast — San Luis Obispo",
    subtitle = "Point color indicates probability of precipitation",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Temperature (°F)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

---

## Exercise 3: Compare Multiple Cities

Write a reusable function, then map it across a list of cities. This is the core pattern for any multi-source data pipeline.

```{r city-function}
get_current_weather <- function(city, key) {
  url <- glue(
    "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather",
    "?q={city}&appid={key}&units=imperial"
  )

  response <- req_perform(request(url))

  if (resp_status(response) != 200) {
    message("Failed for city: ", city)
    return(NULL)
  }

  d <- resp_body_json(response)

  data.frame(
    city        = d$name,
    country     = d$sys$country,
    temp        = d$main$temp,
    feels_like  = d$main$feels_like,
    humidity    = d$main$humidity,
    description = d$weather[[1]]$description,
    wind_speed  = d$wind$speed
  )
}
```

```{r city-map}
cities <- c(
  "San Luis Obispo,US",
  "Chicago,US",
  "Miami,US",
  "Seattle,US",
  "Denver,US"
)

# Map the function across all cities
weather_comparison <- lapply(cities, get_current_weather, key = weather_key) |>
  bind_rows()

print(weather_comparison)
```

```{r city-plot}
ggplot(weather_comparison, aes(x = reorder(city, temp), y = temp, fill = temp)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, radius = unit(4, "pt")) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(temp), "°F")),
            hjust = -0.15, fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#4f46e5", high = "#ef4444") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Current Temperature by City",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Temperature (°F)",
    fill  = "Temp (°F)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

---

## Questions

#### ———————
### Q1. {.unnumbered}

**(Conceptual):** The AQI endpoint requires lat/lon, not a city name. Why do you think some API endpoints work with city names while others require coordinates?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Answer Q1"}
City names are ambiguous — there are many cities named "Springfield" or "Paris." Coordinates are globally unique. Endpoints that measure physical phenomena at a precise geographic point (like air quality or elevation) require coordinates because a city-level approximation would be inaccurate. The geocoding step converts the human-readable name into the precise coordinates the scientific endpoint needs.
:::
#### ———————


#### ———————
### Q2. {.unnumbered}

**(Code):** The 5-day forecast `datetime` column comes from a Unix timestamp (`step$dt`). What does `as_datetime()` from `lubridate` do to it, and what would the raw value look like without that conversion?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Answer Q2"}
`as_datetime()` converts the raw **Unix timestamp** (an integer counting seconds since January 1, 1970) into a human-readable `POSIXct` datetime object. Without conversion, the column would contain numbers like `1704067200` — correct but unreadable and unusable for time-based plotting.
:::
#### ———————


#### ———————
### Q3. {.unnumbered}

**(Extension):** Modify `get_current_weather()` to also retrieve the **air quality index** for each city. You'll need to geocode the city first, then call the AQI endpoint with the coordinates. What challenges do you run into?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Answer Q3"}
The main challenge is **making two API calls per city** instead of one — geocoding (to get lat/lon) and then AQI (using those coordinates). This doubles the number of requests and makes rate limiting more of a concern. A robust solution caches the coordinates and handles errors at each step:

```r
get_weather_and_aqi <- function(city, key) {
  coords <- get_coordinates(city, key)
  weather <- get_current_weather(city, key)

  aqi_url  <- glue("http://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/air_pollution?lat={coords$lat}&lon={coords$lon}&appid={key}")
  aqi_resp <- resp_body_json(req_perform(request(aqi_url)))

  weather$aqi <- aqi_resp$list[[1]]$main$aqi
  weather
}
```
:::
#### ———————
